title: SAA-C03 Daily Mock
subtitle: Day 3 — S3, CloudFormation, CloudWatch, Shared Responsibility, HA/FT/DR, Route 53 & DNS
topics_covered: HA vs Fault Tolerance vs Disaster Recovery

questions.stem: A team needs to store large numbers of files such as images, logs, and backups using a service built specifically for that purpose. Which description matches Amazon S3?

options.text: Object storage that keeps objects in buckets, where each object has a key and its data, scoped to a Region.
options.why: Correct — S3 is Region-based object storage; objects (a key plus data) live inside buckets.

options.text: Block storage that presents raw, unformatted volumes which you attach to a single server and then format and manage yourself.
options.why: Wrong — that describes block storage, not S3's object model.

options.text: A shared file system you mount over the network so many servers can use it at once.
options.why: Wrong — that describes network file storage, not S3.

options.text: A relational database service that stores structured tables you query with SQL.
options.why: Wrong — that describes a database service, not object storage.

questions.stem: An architect is checking whether S3 fits a requirement. For which of the following is S3 the LEAST suitable choice?

options.text: Acting as the boot disk that an instance's operating system runs from.
options.why: Correct — S3 is object storage, not block storage, so it cannot serve as an instance's running OS boot volume.

options.text: Holding a large library of media files that users download over the web.
options.why: Wrong — this is a classic, well-suited use of S3.

options.text: Keeping nightly database backup files for durable long-term retention.
options.why: Wrong — storing backup objects durably is a good fit for S3.

options.text: Storing user-uploaded documents for an application to retrieve later.
options.why: Wrong — storing and retrieving uploaded objects is exactly what S3 is for.

questions.stem: A developer is creating an S3 bucket. Which statement about S3 buckets is correct?

options.text: The bucket name must be globally unique across all AWS accounts, and the bucket resides in one Region.
options.why: Correct — bucket names share a single global namespace, while the bucket itself is created in a specific Region.

options.text: The bucket name only has to be unique within your own account, and the bucket spans every Region.
options.why: Wrong — names are globally unique, and a bucket lives in one Region, not all of them.

options.text: Buckets are created separately within each Availability Zone, and the chosen name only needs to be unique inside that particular zone.
options.why: Wrong — buckets are Region-scoped, not per-AZ, and naming is global.

options.text: Bucket names are generated automatically by AWS and cannot be chosen by the user.
options.why: Wrong — you choose the bucket name (subject to the global-uniqueness and DNS rules).

questions.stem: A team wants to define their AWS infrastructure in a text file and have AWS create and manage those resources from it. Which service is designed for this?

options.text: CloudFormation, which reads a template and provisions the described resources as a stack.
options.why: Correct — CloudFormation turns a template into a managed stack of real resources.

options.text: CloudWatch, which continuously gathers operational metrics and log data and can raise alarms whenever they cross a threshold.
options.why: Wrong — that is a true description of CloudWatch, but it does not provision infrastructure.

options.text: S3, which can store the template file durably as an object inside a bucket.
options.why: Wrong — S3 can hold the file, but it does not create the resources the template describes.

options.text: IAM, which controls which identities are permitted to create the resources.
options.why: Wrong — IAM governs permissions, not the provisioning of the infrastructure itself.

questions.stem: In CloudFormation, what is the relationship between a template and a stack?

options.text: A template describes the resources, and a stack is the set of resources created and managed from that template.
options.why: Correct — the template is the definition; the stack is the live set of resources built from it.

options.text: A stack describes the resources, and a template is the running infrastructure created from the stack.
options.why: Wrong — this reverses the two terms.

options.text: A template and a stack are simply two names for the same single object.
options.why: Wrong — they are distinct: one is the definition, the other is the deployed result.

options.text: A template stores the monitoring data for resources, while a stack keeps the billing and cost records that AWS generates for them over time.
options.why: Wrong — neither stores monitoring or billing data; that is unrelated to CloudFormation's role.

questions.stem: An EC2 instance sends CPU utilization to CloudWatch automatically, but the team also needs memory usage and free disk space from inside the instance. How are those values collected?

options.text: Install the CloudWatch agent on the instance to publish the in-OS metrics.
options.why: Correct — memory and disk are inside the guest OS, so the CloudWatch agent is needed to publish them.

options.text: They are already included automatically in the default set of EC2 metrics.
options.why: Wrong — default EC2 metrics do not include in-OS values like memory or disk usage.

options.text: Turn on detailed monitoring, which adds memory and disk usage at one-minute granularity.
options.why: Wrong — detailed monitoring only increases metric frequency; it does not add in-OS metrics.

options.text: Create a CloudWatch alarm on the instance and the metrics will then appear.
options.why: Wrong — alarms only watch metrics that already exist; they do not generate new ones.

questions.stem: Which TWO of the following are core capabilities of CloudWatch? (Select TWO.)

options.text: Collecting and storing metrics, such as resource utilization measured over time.
options.why: Correct — gathering and retaining metrics is a primary CloudWatch function.

options.text: Ingesting and storing log data from AWS services and from applications.
options.why: Correct — CloudWatch Logs ingests and stores log data, another core capability.

options.text: Provisioning entire sets of infrastructure from a declarative template and then managing them together as a single stack.
options.why: Wrong — that is CloudFormation's job, not CloudWatch's.

options.text: Registering domain names and hosting the DNS records for them.
options.why: Wrong — that is Route 53, not CloudWatch.

options.text: Storing objects durably inside Region-scoped buckets.
options.why: Wrong — that is S3, not CloudWatch.

questions.stem: Under the AWS shared responsibility model, which task is the CUSTOMER's responsibility rather than AWS's?

options.text: Patching the guest operating system and the applications on an EC2 instance.
options.why: Correct — for EC2 the customer manages the guest OS and applications, including patching.

options.text: Maintaining the physical security and environmental controls of the AWS data center facilities.
options.why: Wrong — physical security of facilities is AWS's responsibility (security 'of' the cloud).

options.text: Replacing failed physical disks within the underlying hardware.
options.why: Wrong — the physical hardware is managed entirely by AWS.

options.text: Securing the hypervisor that keeps customer instances isolated.
options.why: Wrong — the virtualization layer is AWS's responsibility, not the customer's.

questions.topic: HA vs Fault Tolerance vs Disaster Recovery
questions.stem: A payment system must keep operating without any interruption even if an individual component fails in the middle of a transaction. Which design goal does this BEST describe?

options.text: Fault tolerance — the system keeps operating through component failures.
options.why: Correct — fault tolerance means continuing to operate with no interruption despite failures.

options.text: High availability — downtime is kept small and recovery happens quickly after a failure.
options.why: Wrong — high availability accepts brief downtime, whereas this requirement allows none.

options.text: Disaster recovery — procedures exist to restore the service after a major event.
options.why: Wrong — disaster recovery is about recovering after an event, not running through one uninterrupted.

options.text: Elasticity — capacity scales automatically up and down with changing demand.
options.why: Wrong — elasticity is a true concept but addresses scaling, not uninterrupted operation.

questions.topic: HA vs Fault Tolerance vs Disaster Recovery
questions.stem: An internal tool may be briefly unavailable during a failure as long as it returns quickly and outages are rare. Which design goal fits this requirement BEST?

options.text: High availability — maximize uptime and recover fast, tolerating short outages.
options.why: Correct — high availability targets minimal downtime while accepting occasional brief outages, matching the requirement.

options.text: Fault tolerance — keep running through failures with no interruption whatsoever.
options.why: Wrong — this is stronger and costlier than needed; the requirement permits short outages, so it is not the best fit.

options.text: Disaster recovery — bring the service back after a significant disaster event.
options.why: Wrong — disaster recovery addresses major events, not routine quick recovery from small failures.

options.text: Durability — guarantee that stored data is not lost over the long term.
options.why: Wrong — durability is a true concept but concerns data loss, not service uptime.

questions.stem: Which statement BEST describes Amazon Route 53?

options.text: A globally resilient service that can register domains and host their DNS records.
options.why: Correct — Route 53 is a global DNS service offering domain registration and hosted zones.

options.text: A Region-scoped service that stores DNS records only within a single Region.
options.why: Wrong — Route 53 is global and globally resilient, not confined to one Region.

options.text: A service that gathers performance metrics and triggers alarms whenever configured thresholds are crossed over time.
options.why: Wrong — that describes CloudWatch, not Route 53.

options.text: A service that stores objects in buckets and serves them out over HTTP.
options.why: Wrong — that describes S3, not a DNS service.

questions.stem: You need a DNS record that points the name 'shop.example.com' to another DNS name, 'web.provider.net', instead of to an IP address. Which record type fits?

options.text: CNAME, which maps a name to another name.
options.why: Correct — a CNAME points one name at another name, which is exactly what is required here.

options.text: A, which maps a name directly to an IPv4 address.
options.why: Wrong — an A record resolves to an IPv4 address, not to another name.

options.text: AAAA, which maps a name directly to an IPv6 address.
options.why: Wrong — an AAAA record resolves to an IPv6 address, not to another name.

options.text: MX, which directs email for a domain toward its mail servers.
options.why: Wrong — MX records route mail and are unrelated to pointing one name at another.

questions.stem: A cloud engineer reviews the default VPC in one Region. Which TWO statements about the default VPC are correct? (Select TWO.)

options.text: Each Region has at most one default VPC, and it can be removed and regenerated later.
options.why: Correct — there is a single default VPC per Region, and it can be deleted and recreated from the console.

options.text: It is ready for connectivity, with an internet gateway and a default subnet in each Availability Zone.
options.why: Correct — the default VPC comes preconfigured with an IGW and a default subnet per AZ.

options.text: A single default VPC is shared across every Region in the account at once.
options.why: Wrong — VPCs are Region-scoped, so a default VPC exists within one Region only.

options.text: Reaching the internet from any of its subnets first requires you to manually create and then attach an internet gateway.
options.why: Wrong — the default VPC already has its internet gateway attached.

options.text: It is the setup AWS recommends for hosting production workloads.
options.why: Wrong — custom VPCs are preferred for production; the default VPC is mainly a convenience.

questions.stem: Which statement about the default VPC's IP addressing is correct?

options.text: It uses a predefined IPv4 CIDR block of 172.31.0.0/16.
options.why: Correct — every default VPC is created with the fixed 172.31.0.0/16 CIDR range.

options.text: It uses a 10.0.0.0/16 block that the account owner selects at creation time.
options.why: Wrong — the default VPC's range is fixed and is not chosen by the user.

options.text: It is created with no CIDR block until one is assigned afterwards.
options.why: Wrong — the default VPC always has its predefined CIDR from the start.

options.text: Its CIDR block shifts automatically to avoid overlapping with other VPCs.
options.why: Wrong — the CIDR is static; AWS does not change it to prevent overlaps.
